jokes.text: News radio reports 'The New York City Snipper' is snipping women's ponytails
jokes.setup: Serious news radio tone establishes a crime story — listeners expect something genuinely threatening
jokes.punchline: The crime is snipping women's ponytails — menacing name, utterly petty act. 'Snipper' is also a near-homophone of 'Sniper'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cold open. 'The NYC Snipper' has perfect economy — it sounds like a serial killer but the crime is absurd and harmless. The sniper/snipper near-homophone is clean wordplay embedded in a concept gag. It also doubles as immediate world-building for this episode's inciting incident. Strong but not legendary.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Abbi 'ma'am' after the subway chaos
jokes.setup: After a chaotic scramble where Ilana missed her train helping Abbi, normal social pleasantries resume
jokes.punchline: Ilana says 'You're welcome, ma'am' — weirdly formal given the chaos, instantly funny because of the tonal whiplash
jokes.explanation: Small but effective tonal beat. Ilana's weirdly polite 'ma'am' after being screwed over cuts perfectly against the preceding chaos. Very Broad City — immediate social recalibration after disaster.

jokes.text: 'Donald Trump will be president!'
jokes.setup: Subway chaos, stressed commuters — someone yells something from offscreen
jokes.punchline: A random person shouts 'Donald Trump will be president!' — treated as the ravings of a lunatic in the context of early Season 4 (set pre-2016 election), but it's a joke that the audience watching knows came true
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The show is explicitly set pre-election and this line lands in multiple registers simultaneously: to characters it's crazy-person subway ranting, to contemporary viewers watching in 2017 it's anguished comedy, and on rewatch it functions as a punchline to history itself. Maximum information density — five words — and the timing inside the chaos makes it hit harder. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: The F Train is suspended — the two strangers (Abbi and Ilana) and a third man all introduce themselves formally amid subway chaos
jokes.setup: Ongoing transit disaster — yet another obstacle
jokes.punchline: Everyone pivots to polite introductions mid-crisis: 'I'm Abbi.' 'I'm Ilana.' 'I'm Robert.' 'Hi, Robert.' 'Hey!' — the mundane social ritual in an absurd context, plus Robert's cheerful 'Hey!' is unexpectedly sweet
jokes.explanation: The formality of introductions during an ongoing disaster is well-observed NYC comedy. Robert's inclusion and enthusiastic 'Hey!' is a perfect throwaway — he's irrelevant to the plot but earns a laugh. This scene functions as the official first meeting of Abbi and Ilana and plays beautifully on rewatch.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I forgot to lock up last night, so then I was supposed to get there five minutes early this morning to fake lock up.'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains why she can't just be a little late to work
jokes.punchline: The plan wasn't to fix the problem — it was to fake having done it. The layered incompetence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Strong character joke. The solution to a mistake is a more elaborate fake-out — this is perfectly Abbi: conscientious enough to feel guilty, chaotic enough to have made it worse. 'Fake lock up' is a great two-word punchline. High character integration score is justified.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Best thing to do right now is to smoke a lot of pot.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just explained her work disaster — the logical next move should be damage control
jokes.punchline: She concludes definitively that massive pot smoking is the correct response
jokes.explanation: Reliable Broad City beat — weed as solution to all problems. Not especially original but delivered with conviction. Sets up Ilana's follow-on line nicely.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I was just gonna go to Bed, Bath and Beyond, but I could definitely do that high.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has proposed smoking a lot of weed. Ilana was planning an errand.
jokes.punchline: Bed, Bath and Beyond stoned is treated as a totally normal and even appealing upgrade to the plan — the casualness is the joke
jokes.explanation: This is a quintessential Ilana line — the utter lack of escalation, the total comfort with doing all errands high. 'But I could definitely do that high' is a great delivery note because of its casual confidence. High character integration: this is entirely Ilana's worldview distilled.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Yeah, I thought that's what you meant.' (re: 'I could smoke you up')
jokes.setup: Abbi offers to 'smoke Ilana up' — Ilana's excited 'Oh, cool!' suggests she may have initially heard something else
jokes.punchline: Ilana admits she assumed it was a sexual overture and is only mildly disappointed that it's weed
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana — her default assumption is everything is sexual and she rolls with the correction gracefully. The 'Oh, cool!' pivot makes the actual line land because we can see the small recalibration. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi gets kicked in the face by a street performer (inferred: she reacts physically, clutches face, gets offered the day's earnings)
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are watching street performers
jokes.punchline: Abbi takes a strong kick to the face — the performer immediately offers her the tip money as compensation
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat — being casually brutalized by street performers is very New York. The money offering is a funny transactional follow-through. Visual payoff depends on the actual kick sell, which the dialogue confirms was significant ('God, that's a strong kick').

jokes.text: Street Performer: 'I'm just a silly, upper-middle class NYU bitch, so I wouldn't take money from you.' Then immediately: 'All right, I'll take 20.'
jokes.setup: Performer does a self-deprecating speech about not needing the money due to her class privilege
jokes.punchline: Instantly takes $20 when the other NYU student commiserates — the performative generosity evaporates immediately
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with sharp social satire. The NYU/class-guilt performance collapsing the second a social in-group connection is made ('We all do. Tischies.') is very specific and very funny. 'All right, I'll take 20' has perfect economy — no hedging, just acceptance.

jokes.text: Man with bubble gun: 'Whoops! Now you're all wet! Even though I know you were already.'
jokes.setup: A man with a bubble gun shoots it at Abbi (in the alternate timeline)
jokes.punchline: He implies she was sexually aroused before he sprayed her — a creepy sexual non sequitur with a bubble gun
jokes.explanation: Works as cringe comedy and satire of street harassment — the absurdity of the bubble gun makes the sexual innuendo simultaneously pathetic and gross, which is the point. Sets up Abbi's response in the alternate timeline.

jokes.text: Alternate-timeline Abbi simply says 'I love my life' after accepting the bubble gun harassment
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been sexually harassed with a bubble gun by a stranger
jokes.punchline: She says 'I love my life' — either deeply ironic or showing a disturbing emotional disconnect from the harassment
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition is excellent: the worst possible response to street harassment delivered with sincerity (or devastating irony). It works on multiple levels — this is the Sliding Doors version of Abbi who had a worse day and has been ground down. Paired with 'I hate my life' later, it forms a structural joke about the two timelines.

jokes.text: In the alternate timeline, the same bubble gun man appears again and Abbi explodes: 'You're a needle-dick bitch, bitch. This guy's a bitch! You know hashtag, winning? You're hashtag, losing. That's your symbol of masculinity, a bubble gun?'
jokes.setup: Same bubble gun harasser, but now Abbi doesn't take it — she unloads completely
jokes.punchline: The rant is so prolonged and specific ('hashtag, losing,' 'needle-dick bitch, bitch') that it becomes absurd and funny. 'That's your symbol of masculinity, a bubble gun?' is the peak.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the bubble gun scene where this timeline's Abbi fights back is deeply satisfying. 'Needle-dick bitch, bitch' is delightfully redundant. The 'hashtag' rhetoric dates it but authentically so. 'Symbol of masculinity, a bubble gun?' gets the biggest laugh of the rant. This scene also functions structurally as the thesis of the Sliding Doors conceit — same situation, different response.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I hate my life.' (callback to 'I love my life' from alternate timeline)
jokes.setup: 'I love my life' was said after passively accepting harassment in the first timeline
jokes.punchline: 'I hate my life' after fighting back — the inversion suggests this timeline is actually worse, even though she stood up for herself
jokes.explanation: This is a structurally elegant joke. Four words that comment on the entire episode's Sliding Doors conceit — the empowered response still leads to misery. It's funny AND meaningful. The economy is perfect (10/10). Justifies the 8+ score: it's doing dramatic and comedic work simultaneously, a genuinely well-crafted television moment.

jokes.text: Ilana: fired from work, and her reaction is 'Come on, dude, fuck Grey Dog, I hate that fucking place!'
jokes.setup: Abbi delivers bad news — she got fired
jokes.punchline: Ilana's supportive reaction is to trash the place Abbi just said she loves
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Ilana — instant fierce loyalty expressed as aggression toward the institution. It sets up the reversal beautifully.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Dude, I love Grey Dog!' / Ilana: '...But the one on Carmine is like, amazing. The staff is so sweet.' / Abbi: 'Dude, that's where I work!' / Ilana: 'Dude, I'm in there like every day!'
jokes.setup: Ilana just trashed Grey Dog to comfort Abbi, then immediately backtracks to praise the Carmine location
jokes.punchline: They've been near-neighbors this whole time — the recognition escalates through both of them simultaneously
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire 'Dude!' escalation works well — it mimics how real people talk when shocked. The situational irony (they've been near each other for years) is well-earned given the episode's Sliding Doors premise. Structural payoff for the mythology of the Abbi-Ilana friendship.

jokes.text: Catering job opened up 'cause some guy just OD'ed'
jokes.setup: Ilana offers Abbi a new job opportunity to replace the one she just lost
jokes.punchline: The spot opened because someone overdosed — delivered with total casualness
jokes.explanation: Classic Broad City dark-casual — life's tragedies treated as mere scheduling logistics. The beat is Abbi's stunned pause after 'OD'ed.' The fact that Ilana moves right past it to 'Dude...' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Abbi's notepad falls, Ilana scrambles to save it — Abbi discovers the Snipper took her ponytail: 'Oh my God, my hair! Oh, this is bad.'
jokes.setup: The NYC Snipper was established at the cold open
jokes.punchline: The Snipper got Abbi — discovered in the middle of an unrelated crisis beat, making it funnier
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff to the cold open. The Snipper getting Abbi mid-scene (while worrying about a notebook) is well-timed. The visual of Abbi realizing her ponytail is gone has strong physical comedy potential.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'No, it looks so good like that. Why do you straighten it?' / Abbi: ''Cause I look like a true Jew if I don't straighten.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's hair is now curly/big after the ponytail was snipped
jokes.punchline: Abbi's explanation for straightening is blunt self-deprecating Jewish-hair commentary
jokes.explanation: 'I look like a true Jew' — specific, self-aware, and funny because it's delivered as plainly as possible. Works as character comedy (establishing Abbi's Jewish identity) and as honest body-image humor.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'It's not necessarily a bad thing.' / Abbi: 'You wouldn't know.' / Ilana: 'No, I'm Jewish.' / Abbi: 'Not full.' / Ilana: 'Yeah, I'm completely Jewish. Check the records. Had a bat mitzvah.'
jokes.setup: Ilana claims to be Jewish; Abbi challenges her Jewishness
jokes.punchline: 'Check the records' and 'Had a bat mitzvah' as documentary proof of Jewishness — the confidence of the defense is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Check the records' is a genuinely funny phrase — legalistic, defensive, and absurdly formal for this context. 'Not full' as a Jewish authenticity challenge is smart. The exchange reads as improv-born but structurally works as an escalating back-and-forth.

jokes.text: Ilana suggests Abbi looks like Rosie Perez with her curly hair, Abbi is offended, then immediately declares she's doing it this way every day forever
jokes.setup: Ilana tries to compliment Abbi's new hair with a celebrity comparison
jokes.punchline: Abbi's initial 'What?' of offense flips completely — 'Now I'm doing it like this every freaking day for the rest of my life'
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — offense flips to complete buy-in instantly. The contrast between her two reactions ('What?' vs. 'every day for the rest of my life') is the engine. Also works as character comedy since it shows how quickly Abbi's self-image can shift.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Bonus Jonas!'
jokes.setup: Abbi realizes her new hairstyle will save her two hours every morning so she'll never be late for work
jokes.punchline: 'Bonus Jonas!' — her exclamation for unexpected bonus benefits, rhyming slang that is deeply specific to Abbi's personal lexicon
jokes.explanation: This is one of the standout jokes of the episode. 'Bonus Jonas' is an invented expression that feels completely real — it's got rhyme, rhythm, and zero logical justification, making it perfectly Abbi. It says everything about her character: dorky, enthusiastic, living in her own vernacular world. Near-perfect character integration (9.5) because this could ONLY be Abbi. Quotability is off the charts — this is the kind of phrase fans repeat. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Abbi/Ilana: 'I'm starving.' / 'Oh my God I thought you'd never ask. Yes, I'm starving too.' / 'Jinx tummies.'
jokes.setup: The girls agree to get breakfast
jokes.punchline: 'Jinx tummies' — Abbi's invented childlike expression for synchronized hunger
jokes.explanation: 'Jinx tummies' is in the same vein as 'Bonus Jonas' — Abbi's invented language is a strong comedy vein. It's a baby version of 'jinx' applied to physical hunger, and it works because of how earnestly it's delivered. High quotability.

jokes.text: Abbi's ponytail is swiped by the Snipper in the other timeline — discovered as everyone panics: 'He took my pony!'
jokes.setup: NYC Snipper established in cold open; ponytail snipping has been a running threat
jokes.punchline: Abbi finally gets snipped — announced with 'He took my pony!' which is both literal and sounds childlike/alarming
jokes.explanation: 'He took my pony!' works because 'pony' sounds both like a horse and like a child's toy, adding absurdity to the trauma. The callback delivery works in this timeline.

jokes.text: Ilana is caught drinking the Kenyan hazelnut coffee at work and fired — her response: 'Am I at least entitled to my one free coffee a day for employees?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is fired for stealing coffee (and presumably other ongoing issues)
jokes.punchline: Her response to being fired is to immediately claim a benefit she invented
jokes.explanation: One of the best character comedy moments in the episode. Ilana, upon being fired, immediately pivots to negotiating a benefit that doesn't exist. The confidence is total. Her boss's 'You made that up. There is no one free coffee a day for employees. You're just a thief.' is the perfect straight-man response. Justifies 8+: the joke is entirely about character — it could ONLY be Ilana, and it reveals her worldview completely.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Wow, did you just call me a queef?' (mishearing 'thief')
jokes.setup: Tim says 'You're just a thief' and tells her to leave
jokes.punchline: Ilana mishears 'thief' as 'queef' and claims sexual harassment
jokes.explanation: 'Thief/queef' is a solid near-homophone gag. What makes it great is Ilana immediately weaponizing it as a sexual harassment claim — it's not just a mishearing, it's an attempt to survive getting fired. Pure Ilana: escalating absurdity as a survival mechanism. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana, freshly fired: 'Yeah, I'm so excited. First week in New York, I love this city.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just been fired and essentially thrown out of her job
jokes.punchline: She responds with genuine enthusiasm about being in New York — either completely ironic or completely sincere, both are funny
jokes.explanation: This line retroactively reveals this is a flashback/origin story — 'First week in New York' is a significant worldbuilding drop. The fact that getting fired is her 'I love this city' moment is pure Ilana characterization. Also recontextualizes the whole cold open timeline.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'All my friends call me Bevers.'
jokes.setup: Abbi meets her roommate's boyfriend — he introduces himself as Matt
jokes.punchline: 'All my friends call me Bevers' — a nickname that sounds like a slur but is just his name, delivered with pride
jokes.explanation: Great introductory joke for a recurring character. 'Bevers' as a nickname is inherently funny (rhymes with 'beavers') and its delivery with complete pride tells you everything about this character immediately. The origin of Bevers as a recurring character makes this a foundational joke.

jokes.text: Bevers explains the house rules: 'as a guest, the rules of the house are I never pay for food, I never do chores' — Abbi corrects him: 'No, that'd be weird.' Bevers: 'Okay. Got it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says 'make yourself at home' and tries to be a good host
jokes.punchline: Bevers confidently summarizes his total freeloading as the host's stated 'rules' — and accepts the correction cheerfully, implying he'll ignore it
jokes.explanation: Bevers immediately establishes himself as the consummate freeloader with total sincerity. His misrepresentation of Abbi's hospitality as official house rules, and his breezy 'Okay, got it' when corrected, is perfectly written cringe comedy. The audience knows he will absolutely not follow the correction.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Eww, I am having, like, a salty, wet diarrhea of a fucking day!'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives to find her suitemates having a meeting without her
jokes.punchline: 'A salty, wet diarrhea of a fucking day' — an extremely vivid and grossly specific metaphor for a bad day
jokes.explanation: 'Salty, wet diarrhea of a fucking day' is an exceptional line. The adjectives are maximally specific ('salty, wet') and the noun is perfectly wrong — it sounds like it's about to be poetic and lands in pure bodily disgust. This is Ilana's voice at its highest. Could only be her. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Why would you have a suite-ing without me?'
jokes.setup: Ilana discovers her suitemates are having a suite meeting without her
jokes.punchline: 'Suite-ing' — a pun on 'meeting' using 'suite', delivered as a genuine grievance
jokes.explanation: 'Suite-ing' is a clean, economical pun. The indignation about the exclusion is funnier than the wordplay itself. Slight bonus for how naturally it's deployed — it doesn't feel labored.

jokes.text: Grievance #27 in the suite PowerPoint: 'You call all of us Madison.'
jokes.setup: The suite is running a formal PowerPoint presentation of grievances against Ilana
jokes.punchline: Ilana calls all her suitemates 'Madison' — and her instinctive reaction ('What are you talking about, Madis—') proves the point mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: This is the best-crafted joke in the episode. The setup (formal PowerPoint grievance), punchline (she calls them all Madison), and instant self-proof (she starts saying 'What are you talking about, Madis—') form a perfect comedic trifecta. It's structurally immaculate: the joke proves itself in real time. Ilana can't help but demonstrate the complaint while objecting to it. This is professional-level comedy writing. Justifies 9+.

jokes.text: Grievance: 'The wall of graphic pornography above your bed.' Ilana: 'Oh, come on! I contribute a lot of culture to this suite.'
jokes.setup: Suite grievances continue — this one is about pornography on the wall
jokes.punchline: Ilana defends graphic porn as 'culture' — a completely sincere defense that's unhinged
jokes.explanation: 'I contribute a lot of culture to this suite' as a defense of pornography is well-calibrated Ilana. The grievance itself is funny but the cultural defense is funnier.

jokes.text: Suitemate: 'You're always making us watch videos of animals having sex with people.' Ilana: 'Oh my God, again! I showed you ONE video. Many times. Of a man fighting for the sensual pleasure of his life, only to die later from internal bleeding. It's Mr. Hands!'
jokes.setup: The pornography grievance escalates — it's not just porn, it's bestiality snuff content
jokes.punchline: 'It's Mr. Hands!' — referencing a real and infamous internet video with the enthusiasm of someone defending their favorite film
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'pornography on wall' to 'bestiality snuff content we watched many times' is perfectly paced. 'A man fighting for the sensual pleasure of his life, only to die later from internal bleeding' is an extraordinary euphemistic description. 'It's Mr. Hands!' as a triumphant reveal is dark comedy gold. High impact because of the shock of the escalation.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I see... because if it were a lady horse and a man, no one would have a problem with it. They can get fucking married for all I care. But gay dudes can't get married? Gay dudes can't get married, so a horse-dude and a dude-dude can not fuck.'
jokes.setup: Suitemates object to the bestiality content
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes bestiality as a gender/marriage equality issue — constructing an internal logic that is insane but structurally coherent
jokes.explanation: Ilana constructs a genuine political argument for why the bestiality video is acceptable by tieing it to marriage equality. The internal logic is airtight: 'gay dudes can't get married, so a horse-dude and a dude-dude cannot fuck.' It's absurdist but structured, which makes it genuinely impressive. 'Horse-dude and dude-dude' is exceptional terminology. This is Ilana at peak Ilana. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Grievance about Ilana's curly hair: 'When your hair is curly, it looks like pubes.' Ilana: 'You're a fucking bitch, but I'll go over it with the iron again.'
jokes.setup: Suite grievances list a personal appearance complaint about Ilana's hair
jokes.punchline: Ilana agrees to address it while simultaneously insulting the person who raised it
jokes.explanation: 'You're a fucking bitch, but I'll go over it with the iron again' perfectly captures Ilana — she accepts the criticism and rejects the person simultaneously. The pragmatic capitulation plus insult is a funny character-specific response.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I am so relieved that we have a hot black guy as president. I mean, I feel like we're finally moving forward. And it's just the beginning. I mean, never backwards. Only forwards! Next is a woman. Whoo!'
jokes.setup: Discussion of the Obamas in context of a throuple fantasy
jokes.punchline: Ilana's optimism about 'never backwards, only forwards' and 'next is a woman' is devastating dramatic irony — the audience in 2017 knows exactly what came next
jokes.explanation: Paired with the 'Donald Trump will be president' subway moment from the cold open, this is the second half of an episode-long structural joke about historical dramatic irony. 'Never backwards. Only forwards! Next is a woman. Whoo!' is gut-punch comedy — Ilana's pure optimism resonates painfully because the audience knows 2016 happened. The impact score of 9.5 reflects the reaction this likely got in Season 4 premiere audiences. Structurally it's also the episode's thesis: the Sliding Doors of history. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Abbi arrives with a black eye; Ilana: 'Aye, no, did you get kicked in the face by a subway performer again?'
jokes.setup: The subway performer kick from earlier in the episode
jokes.punchline: 'Again' — implying this has happened before, which is either a callback to this episode or a running pattern in their friendship
jokes.explanation: 'Again' is doing a lot of work — it confirms Abbi's general disaster-magnetism as an established character trait and is both a callback and a running gag implication. Ilana's immediate correct diagnosis is a nice friendship shorthand joke.

jokes.text: Ilana is suddenly called to present in class — she hasn't prepared; she tries to get Jaime to pull the fire alarm. Jaime: 'I really want to, but I'm trying to become a citizen one day. It's illegal.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to escape giving a presentation she hasn't prepared
jokes.punchline: Jaime's refusal is logistically reasonable and delivered completely straight — it's not dramatic, just a practical immigration consideration
jokes.explanation: Jaime's response is perfectly calibrated — completely sincere, with zero judgment of Ilana's request. 'I'm trying to become a citizen one day' as the reason he won't commit arson is both character-revealing and unexpectedly touching. Great deadpan delivery implied by the writing.

jokes.text: 'Jaime, can you pull the fire alarm again?' 'I really want to, but I'm trying to become a citizen one day. It's illegal.' 'I understand.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to escape the class presentation and whispers to Jaime
jokes.punchline: Jaime's refusal is entirely about his immigration status — he would love to pull the alarm, he's clearly done it before ('again'), but citizenship aspirations are the specific reason he can't break this particular law
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke for multiple reasons. 'Again' implies Jaime has pulled fire alarms for Ilana before — same economy move as id 30. The specific reason he can't (immigration/citizenship) grounds the absurdity in real stakes and is both funny and touching. 'I really want to, but...' shows he's a willing accomplice constrained by circumstance. 'I understand' from Ilana — accepting this without question — completes it perfectly. High economy, high character integration, earned rather than cheap.

jokes.text: Ilana begins her presentation: 'Welcome to my lecture. The day is Thursday, the time is one o'clock.' Professor: 'Gotta stop you there. It's Friday and it's 4:00 p.m.'
jokes.setup: Ilana begins her unprepared presentation with false confidence
jokes.punchline: She's wrong about the day AND time, and the professor corrects her immediately
jokes.explanation: The joke is Ilana's total unawareness of basic temporal reality, delivered with complete self-assurance. 'I stand corrected' after being wrong about both the day and the time is a great deadpan response. Confirms she has absolutely no idea what's happening.

jokes.text: Ilana's presentation begins: '♪ White humans / Evil to the bone ♪'
jokes.setup: After being corrected on date and time, Ilana launches her 'presentation'
jokes.punchline: It appears to be a musical performance about white people being evil — not an academic presentation at all
jokes.explanation: Starting an academic presentation with a political folk song about white people being evil is peak Ilana. The professor's weary 'Miss Wexler, we've gone over this' implies this is not the first time. Strong absurdist character comedy.

jokes.text: Professor: 'This is not Tisch.' Ilana: 'Yeah, you made that very clear the day you took my djembe away.'
jokes.setup: Professor shuts down Ilana's musical presentation
jokes.punchline: Ilana has had a djembe confiscated — implying she brought a drum to class and played it, and this has been adjudicated
jokes.explanation: 'The day you took my djembe away' implies a whole prior incident. The professor still has it. The djembe is an African hand drum — the implied image of Ilana bringing a djembe to class and being made to surrender it is beautifully absurd. 'You'll get that back at the end of the semester' as a matter-of-fact follow-on is perfect.

jokes.text: Suitemate fake emergency: 'There's a sinkhole in my hometown. My house is gone!' Ilana: 'Yes, girl, thank you.' Other suitemate: 'I'm serious, you bitch!'
jokes.setup: Ilana told a suitemate to fake an emergency to get her out of presenting
jokes.punchline: The 'fake' emergency turns out to be real — the suitemate's house actually fell into a sinkhole. Ilana's enthusiastic 'yes girl' response makes her look monstrous.
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection — the audience sets up the 'fake emergency' expectation, and it's reversed by being real. 'I'm serious, you bitch!' is a great stinger. Ilana's enthusiastic 'yes girl, thank you' to actual disaster is dark and funny. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Psychic Oda: 'Welcome.' / Abbi: 'Hi, we're interested in—' / Oda: 'Oh, I know. I possess the gift.' / Abbi/Ilana: 'Oh. She's good.'
jokes.setup: They walk into a psychic parlor and she says 'I know' to their opening line
jokes.punchline: The girls are immediately convinced of her psychic powers by the most basic cold-reading trick — she twice says 'I possess the gift' with total gravitas
jokes.explanation: 'I possess the gift' repeated twice is a funny bit of grandiose self-declaration. The girls being immediately bamboozled is consistent with their characters. Functional but not especially original.

jokes.text: Psychic says 'I'm feeling... you girls... are new friends.' Then burps/has indigestion mid-prophecy.
jokes.setup: Psychic has established her mystical gravitas; she begins a prophecy
jokes.punchline: She interrupts her own prophecy with mundane indigestion — immediately apologizes
jokes.explanation: Mystical pretension punctured by physical human weakness is a reliable formula. Works in context because she was clearly eating a burrito bowl and the indigestion is both visual and physical. Sets up Abbi's 'She was eating a burrito bowl' callback.

jokes.text: Psychic: 'Prophecy. You're gonna die today.'
jokes.setup: After the indigestion interruption, the psychic resumes her prophecy
jokes.punchline: The prophecy, delivered in a single flat beat, is that they'll die today
jokes.explanation: The two-word delivery after the indigestion interruption — 'Prophecy. You're gonna die today.' — is perfectly timed. The flatness of the delivery against the stakes is excellent deadpan. Economy score is high because the joke is essentially five words.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'We are so not dying today! So not! I mean, what, are we gonna listen to her? She was eating a burrito bowl.'
jokes.setup: They've just been told they'll die today
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dismissal of the prophecy is based solely on the fact that the psychic was eating a burrito bowl — as if the food choice invalidates the supernatural claim
jokes.explanation: 'She was eating a burrito bowl' as the definitive evidence against psychic credibility is a brilliant non-sequitur. The specific food item (burrito bowl, not 'lunch') is doing real work here. Abbi's logic is both completely wrong and completely understandable.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I don't know, then you can't be buried in the cemetery and that whole thing.' Ilana: 'Yeah, but that's just for Jewish people.' Abbi: 'Yeah, I'm Jewish, remember? I told you.' Ilana: 'I thought you were joking.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's Jewish identity was established in the hair conversation earlier
jokes.punchline: Ilana thought Abbi being Jewish was a joke — implying either that Abbi doesn't 'seem' Jewish enough to Ilana, or that Ilana doesn't fully process information
jokes.explanation: Callback that also reinforces Ilana as someone who doesn't always absorb information. 'I thought you were joking' is a slight on Abbi's Jewish identity or Ilana's attention span — either reading is funny.

jokes.text: Abbi gets an Oprah tattoo
jokes.setup: They decide to get tattoos to live like they're dying
jokes.punchline: Abbi's tattoo of maximum personal significance is Oprah
jokes.explanation: The Oprah tattoo works as an act of sincere devotion that is simultaneously absurd. 'I can't believe she's ending her show' / 'Probably watch the Oprah Winfrey Network' sets up the in-universe logic. The tattoo as a memorial to Oprah's show is deeply Abbi — earnest beyond reason.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I always smoke on the median of that intersection, because when a cop sees me, I just throw my bowl into traffic.'
jokes.setup: Ilana mentions she smokes at a specific intersection
jokes.punchline: Her solution to potential arrest is to throw her glass pipe directly into moving traffic
jokes.explanation: 'I just throw my bowl into traffic' is one of the best Ilana lines in the episode. The specificity (glass bowl, into TRAFFIC, not just away), the casualness of delivery, and Abbi's perfectly calibrated 'You throw a glass bowl into traffic?' are all exactly right. High character integration: this is an insane but internally consistent Ilana solution. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'He sounds like a freeloading bag-of-trash bitch.' 'I mean, I barely said anything about him. I haven't even met him yet.'
jokes.setup: Abbi mentions her roommate's boyfriend is staying with them
jokes.punchline: Ilana renders a comprehensive negative verdict — 'freeloading bag-of-trash bitch' — based on essentially no information; Abbi notes she's barely said anything about him
jokes.explanation: Solid Ilana character moment. The insult is specific and compound ('freeloading bag-of-trash bitch'), and Abbi's pointing out the lack of evidence is the necessary straight-man beat. The insult is immediately validated by Bevers' earlier scenes. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You gotta call your roommate right the fucking now and nip this in the ass.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is giving Abbi urgent advice about Bevers
jokes.punchline: 'Nip this in the ass' — the malapropism of 'nip it in the bud' is deployed with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: 'Nip it in the ass' is a malapropism that somehow works better than the original. Abbi's gentle check — 'Nip it in the ass, is that the saying?' — and Ilana's confident 'Yeah, nip it in the ass' doubling down is perfect. This is very Ilana: wrong with total authority.

jokes.text: Abbi follows Ilana's advice and calls Melody, then Ilana starts coaching mid-call: 'And she should break up with him.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is calling to ask that Bevers leave within the week — a reasonable request
jokes.punchline: Ilana escalates the demand to include a full relationship breakup for Melody
jokes.explanation: Ilana's inability to stay in her lane is a good character beat — she's coaching a call about a roommate situation and immediately advises ending the relationship entirely. Escalation of the stakes mid-bit.

jokes.text: The call goes amazingly well — Abbi gushes: 'We're really communicating. I really thought this would be harder.'
jokes.setup: Abbi expected confrontation about Bevers
jokes.punchline: She's over-praising Melody for basically normal human communication — the relief is disproportionate to the event
jokes.explanation: Abbi's over-effusive reaction to a successful phone call is true to character — she's been anxious about this all episode and the relief comes out as slightly pathetic over-praise. Warm and character-consistent.

jokes.text: Robert: 'I just bought three slices of pizza that I'm gonna eat myself, so... pretty much sums it up.' Abbi: 'Respect.'
jokes.setup: Robert is asked how his day was
jokes.punchline: He's going home to eat three slices alone — his summary of a terrible day. Abbi's 'Respect' validates the solitary pizza as self-care.
jokes.explanation: 'Three slices of pizza that I'm gonna eat myself' as the complete encapsulation of a bad day is relatable and funny. 'Respect' from Abbi elevates it — she doesn't pity him, she honors the decision. Good character beat for both.

jokes.text: 'Do you want a hit?' [offers weed] 'Yeah. That'd be awesome.' 'Thanks. Cool pipe.' 'Got it on McDougal.'
jokes.setup: Two strangers awkwardly reconnecting at the end of their separate days
jokes.punchline: 'Got it on McDougal' — the pipe's provenance is offered as a complete and satisfying conversation item; the NYC street specificity makes it funny
jokes.explanation: Small texture joke. 'Got it on McDougal' as a complete answer to 'cool pipe' is very New York — the specific street is a real location (McDougal Street, where head shops exist). Works as ambient character comedy.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'We should press our breasts into it.' (about wet cement)
jokes.setup: They find wet cement — a rare NYC moment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's immediate suggestion is to press their breasts into it instead of the conventional name-writing
jokes.explanation: 'We should press our breasts into it' is a perfect Broad City moment — slightly raunchy, completely sincere, unexpectedly specific. Ilana's 'We could just write our names, right? Classy. Yeah.' is an excellent reversal — Ilana being the voice of restraint. High character integration because the breast-pressing impulse feels authentically Abbi at her most uninhibited.

jokes.text: Both write their names: 'Abbi with I.' / 'Ilana with an I.' — both names start with the same letter, making the clarification useless
jokes.setup: They're writing their names in wet cement — a significant friendship moment
jokes.punchline: Both helpfully specify 'with an I' — but both their names start with I, making the clarification meaningless
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed joke that works on multiple levels: it's funny as a linguistic redundancy, it speaks to both characters' endearing cluelessness, and it's touching as the founding document of their friendship. The simultaneous 'with an I' from both is charming. It also works as a thematic button — these two are the same. Economy near-perfect. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Abbi has a black eye; Robert asks about it. Abbi: 'You know, I'm a supporter of the arts.'
jokes.setup: Robert notices Abbi's black eye from the subway performer kick
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a supporter of the arts' — a perfectly constructed euphemism that is technically accurate
jokes.explanation: This is the single best line in the episode. 'I'm a supporter of the arts' as an explanation for a black eye from being kicked in the face by a subway performer is perfect — technically true, wildly misrepresentative, and quotable in almost any context. It's a great callback, a great character line (Abbi's self-deprecation masking her embarrassment), and an extremely economical joke. The ratio of information to words is extraordinary. Justifies the high score clearly — it's comparable to the 8.0 anchor example in terms of economy and quotability, and arguably exceeds it.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'That's dope.' (to 'I'm a supporter of the arts') — then: 'You look like an artist, because of your haircut. Did you get it cut today?' Abbi: 'In a way, I did.'
jokes.setup: Robert notices Abbi's haircut (which was actually snipped by the Snipper)
jokes.punchline: 'In a way, I did' — technically accurate about her ponytail being snipped, delivered with perfect deadpan
jokes.explanation: 'In a way, I did' is a masterful callback to the Snipper storyline. It's technically accurate — her hair WAS cut today, just involuntarily. The callback resolves the Snipper thread with grace. High economy, strong structure.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'You know what I've been thinking about lately? This is so, like, stupid, but why can't you get eggs after 11:00 a.m.?' / Robert: 'I think about that all the time!'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Robert are getting to know each other
jokes.punchline: The 'deep thought' Abbi has been sitting on is the eggs-after-11 question — deeply relatable and completely mundane presented as a major concern. Robert's mirror response confirms a connection.
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing beat — two strangers connecting over a shared breakfast-hour grievance. 'Are you serious?' mirrors how both characters can't believe someone shares this very specific concern. Warm, relatable, and a nice romantic-comedy button. 'I think about that all the time!' is the right reaction energy.
